learn.title: Concentrated Solar Power (CSP)
learn.intro: Concentrated Solar Power uses mirrors to focus sunlight, creating heat that drives turbines for electricity generation. This technology enables 24/7 power through thermal storage, bridging the gap between solar availability and energy demand. With global capacity reaching 5.5 GW in 2023, CSP is pivotal for grid stability in sunny regions like the Middle East and North Africa.
learn.c1_text: CSP systems use parabolic troughs or heliostats to reflect sunlight onto a receiver. For example, Morocco's Noor Ouarzazate plant uses 580,000 mirrors to generate 580 MW. These mirrors concentrate sunlight 100-1,000 times, achieving temperatures over 500°C to produce steam for electricity.
learn.c2_text: CSP plants store heat in molten salt tanks, enabling power generation after sunset. The Crescent Dunes plant in Nevada stores 500 tons of salt at 560°C, providing 10 hours of electricity. This 110 MW facility demonstrates how CSP supports nighttime energy needs without batteries.
learn.c3_text: India's National CSP Mission targets 2,650 MW by 2026. The 100 MW Kurnool Ultra Mega Solar Park in Andhra Pradesh uses CSP to complement PV solar. With 300 sunny days annually, Rajasthan and Gujarat offer ideal conditions for CSP deployment under India's Renewable Energy Targets.
learn.stat3: Crescent Dunes Storage: 10 hours of power

learn.title: Small Wind Turbines for Homes
learn.intro: Small wind turbines (under 100 kW) convert local wind into electricity for homes and communities. They reduce grid dependence and emissions, with over 1.5 million units installed globally. In India, where wind potential exceeds 150 GW, these turbines complement solar in rural electrification efforts.
learn.c1_text: Turbines capture wind kinetic energy via blades that spin a generator. A 10 kW turbine in Rajasthan's Jaisalmer produces 20,000 kWh/year, powering 15 homes. Vertical-axis turbines handle turbulent winds better, ideal for Indian villages with irregular wind patterns.
learn.c2_text: Horizontal-axis turbines (HAWT) dominate utility-scale, while vertical-axis (VAWT) suit residential use. In Tamil Nadu, 5 kW VAWTs power 100+ households in cooperative projects. These turbines require 3-5 m/s wind speeds, achievable in India's coastal and hill regions.
learn.c3_text: India's Ministry of New and Renewable Energy promotes small wind under its 500 GW non-fossil target. The Gujarat Energy Development Agency installed 1,000 small turbines in rural areas, cutting diesel costs by 40%. Policy incentives include accelerated depreciation for small wind projects.
learn.stat1: Global Small Wind Installations: 1.5 million units

learn.title: Climate Finance & COP Agreements
learn.intro: Climate finance mobilizes $100+ billion annually for developing nations to transition to renewables. COP agreements like the Paris Agreement bind nations to fund mitigation/adaptation. India leveraged these frameworks to secure $2.5 billion for solar projects under the Green Climate Fund, accelerating its 500 GW renewable target.
learn.c1_text: The Paris Agreement requires developed nations to provide $100 billion/year by 2025 for climate action. India pledged net-zero emissions by 2070, accessing $2.5 billion from the Green Climate Fund. These funds support rooftop solar in Tamil Nadu and mangrove restoration in Odisha.
learn.c2_text: The GCF channels funds to developing countries via projects like India's $500 million solar initiative in Karnataka. It also supports adaptation, such as drought-resilient solar pumps in Maharashtra. To date, GCF has approved $2.3 billion for 200+ projects in Asia-Pacific regions.
learn.c3_text: India received $1.2 billion from GCF for renewable energy in 2023, enabling 10 GW solar capacity. The National Clean Energy Fund leverages COP pledges, with $10 billion allocated for 2024-25. These funds align with India's 2070 net-zero commitment and 500 GW non-fossil target.

learn.title: Sodium-Ion Batteries
learn.intro: Sodium-ion batteries offer a low-cost, abundant alternative to lithium-ion, using sodium from seawater. With 20% lower raw material costs, they're ideal for grid storage. CATL's 2023 launch of sodium-ion cells with 160 Wh/kg signals a shift toward sustainable energy storage, critical for India's 500 GW renewable expansion.
learn.c1_text: Sodium-ion batteries use sodium ions moving between electrode layers. Unlike lithium, sodium is 2,600 times more abundant in Earth's crust. A 100 kWh sodium-ion system costs $8,000 vs. $12,000 for lithium-ion. China's CATL produces 30 GWh/year, targeting 150 GWh by 2025 for global markets.
learn.c2_text: Sodium-ion cells operate safely at -20°C to 60°C without thermal runaway risks. Their 160 Wh/kg energy density suits stationary storage, though lower than lithium-ion's 250 Wh/kg. India's Tata Power plans to deploy 5 GWh sodium-ion capacity by 2027 for solar grid integration.
learn.c3_text: India's Department of Science launched a $100 million sodium-ion R&D program in 2023. IIT Madras developed indigenous cells with 140 Wh/kg, targeting 2025 commercialization. The Ministry aims to reduce battery import dependency (currently 90%) through sodium-ion manufacturing under the PLI scheme.

learn.title: Wind Resource Assessment
learn.intro: Wind resource assessment identifies optimal sites using data on wind speed, direction, and turbulence. Accurate assessments ensure project viability, with India's 60 GW wind capacity relying on maps from the National Institute of Wind Energy. Developers use 12-month anemometer data to predict annual energy output, minimizing financial risks.
learn.c1_text: Assessors deploy anemometers and wind vanes for 12+ months to measure wind parameters. In India's Gujarat coast, 10 m/s average winds yield 25% capacity factors. LiDAR systems provide 3D wind mapping for complex terrains, reducing uncertainty by 15% in mountainous regions like Himachal Pradesh.
learn.c2_text: GIS platforms like Windographer analyze wind shear and turbulence intensity. India's National Wind Resource Assessment uses 1 km resolution models to identify zones with >6 m/s winds. Developers apply WAsP software to estimate annual output: a 100 MW project in Tamil Nadu requires 7.5 m/s winds for 30% capacity factor viability.
learn.c3_text: India's 2023 wind atlas refined predictions using 5 years of satellite data, correcting earlier 30% overestimations. The updated map highlights 21 GW untapped potential in Rajasthan and Gujarat. States like Maharashtra now mandate 6-month assessments before auctions, improving project success rates under the 500 GW target.

learn.title: Solid-State Batteries
learn.intro: Solid-state batteries replace liquid electrolytes with ceramics or polymers, enhancing safety and energy density. With 500 Wh/kg potential (vs. 250 Wh/kg for lithium-ion), they're revolutionizing EVs and grid storage. Toyota's 2025 prototype targets 700 km range, while India's Olectra partners with domestic firms to localize production under the PLI scheme.
learn.c1_text: Solid electrolytes prevent thermal runaway, a key lithium-ion flaw. A solid-state cell can endure 10,000 cycles vs. 3,000 for lithium-ion. QuantumScape's prototype achieved 500 Wh/kg at 80°C, enabling 700 km EV range. These batteries also operate from -30°C to 100°C, ideal for India's climate extremes.
learn.c2_text: Sulfide-based solid electrolytes offer high ionic conductivity (10⁻² S/cm), comparable to liquids. Perovskite ceramics like LLZO provide dendrite resistance. Samsung's 2024 foldable phone battery uses a polymer electrolyte, delivering 1,000 cycles. These materials enable faster charging: 80% in 10 minutes versus 30 minutes for lithium-ion.
learn.c3_text: India's PLI scheme allocated $2.5 billion for advanced battery manufacturing, targeting 50 GWh/year by 2030. IIT Delhi's collaboration with Toyota aims to establish a solid-state plant in Hyderabad. Startups like SFL Aero are developing sodium-based solid electrolytes, reducing reliance on imported lithium and cobalt.
